bullets.text: U.S. Q4 2025 productivity rose 1.8% annualized, but unit labor costs jumped 4.4%, reinforcing sticky services inflation risk.
U.S. reportedly sent Iran a 15-point plan to end the Middle East war, signaling potential diplomatic off-ramp despite mixed public messages.
Iran signaled “non-hostile” ships can transit Hormuz, implying a selective disruption strategy that could still reprice global energy and shipping risk.
Oil swung sharply (down ~5–6%, then back above $100 Brent) as markets alternated between de-escalation hopes and renewed conflict concerns.
A weak Treasury auction highlighted rising term-premium anxiety and war-risk sensitivity, potentially tightening financial conditions even without Fed hikes.
TotalEnergies warned refining margins are at unprecedented highs, suggesting downstream bottlenecks—not just crude supply—are driving fuel price pressure.
Russia launched a daytime strike with 500+ drones, underscoring accelerating drone-war scale and air-defense attrition dynamics in Ukraine.
Denmark’s prime minister gained politically after resisting U.S. pressure on Greenland, highlighting sovereignty disputes as an electoral force in Europe.
Jury ordered Meta to pay $375M for child-safety violations, a landmark courtroom loss that may accelerate platform liability and compliance costs.
OpenAI is shutting down Sora, showing rapid product churn and heightened safety/operational constraints around consumer AI video generation.
Arm launched its first in-house CPU with Meta as a debut customer, moving Arm into direct silicon competition and reshaping AI compute supply chains.
AI hardware demand remains intense: SK Hynix filed confidentially for a U.S. listing, spotlighting investor appetite for memory suppliers.
Moody’s cut a KKR-linked private credit fund to junk amid rising bad loans, signaling growing stress in illiquid credit and refinancing risk.
Research suggests oral insulin pills may replace injections, potentially expanding adherence and reshaping diabetes drug delivery economics.
